turns.student: What brings you in today?
turns.patient: I've had a persistent cough for about five days now, and it's been getting worse. It started off dry, but over the past couple of days, I've noticed it's producing yellowish-green sputum when I cough. The OTC medication I'm taking hasn't really helped much, so I'm getting worried that there might be something more serious going on.

turns.student: Can you tell me more about what you've been feeling?
turns.patient: It's not just the cough that's been bothering me. I've also been feeling really tired and sluggish, like I'm dragging myself out of bed every morning. And sometimes when I breathe in deeply or exert myself, I get this wheezing sound - it sounds almost like I'm asthmatic or something.

turns.student: When you have those episodes, do you notice anything else happening to your body?
turns.patient: Yes, actually. When that wheezing starts, my chest feels a bit tight and I can feel my heart beating faster than usual. It's not excruciating pain or anything, but it's definitely uncomfortable.

turns.student: Any sweating or clamminess when the symptoms hit?
turns.patient: Yes, actually - I've been having some pretty intense night sweats over the past four nights. They're soaking through my pajamas every time, and it's been really unsettling.

turns.student: So no perspiration at all during the episodes?
turns.patient: No, none that I can notice. It's only the night sweats that are concerning me right now. They seem to be unrelated to when the wheezing and coughing happen.

turns.student: Does the discomfort stay in one spot, or does it move around?
turns.patient: The chest tightness seems to stay in my chest area, mostly in the center of my torso. It doesn't really radiate outwards or move to other parts of my body when I'm having those episodes.

turns.student: Does it radiate anywhere — like your arms, neck, or jaw?
turns.patient: No, it seems to stay mostly in my chest area. When I breathe in deeply and get that wheezing sound, the tightness is still just in my center torso, not spreading outwards to any other parts of my body like my arms, neck, or jaw.

turns.student: Any tingling or numbness in your arms when it happens?
turns.patient: No, I don't experience any tingling or numbness in my arms when I'm having those episodes. It's just the chest tightness and wheezing that I've mentioned before.

turns.student: What about your extremities — any tingling, pain, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: No, nothing like that in my arms or legs. Like I said, it stays mostly in my chest area when those episodes happen.

turns.student: Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Ankles, feet, hands?
turns.patient: No, I don't think so. But to be honest, with all these symptoms going on, I'm just trying to keep track of what's happening and when. The cough, wheezing, chest tightness – it's all been pretty overwhelming for me.

turns.student: Do your shoes feel tighter than usual, or any puffiness in your legs?
turns.patient: I'm not sure about my shoes feeling tighter... to be honest, I haven't really paid attention to that. But as for puffiness in my legs, no, I don't think so. Like I said, it's been hard for me to keep track of everything that's going on with these symptoms.

turns.student: Any stomach issues during the episodes? Nausea, queasiness, upset stomach?
turns.patient: I haven't really experienced any significant stomach issues or nausea when I'm having those wheezing and coughing episodes. But to be honest, I have been feeling a bit more tired than usual and my appetite has been off lately, so I've just been eating light meals and trying to keep myself hydrated.

turns.student: Do you feel sick to your stomach when the symptoms come on?
turns.patient: No, I haven't really experienced any significant stomach issues or nausea when I'm having those wheezing and coughing episodes. Like I said, my appetite has been off lately, so I've just been eating light meals and trying to keep myself hydrated.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: Yes, actually. I've had a low-grade fever, around 38.2°C (100.8°F), that comes and goes. It's been intermittent, but it tends to get worse in the evenings. And like I mentioned earlier, I've also been having some pretty intense night sweats over the past four nights.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your appetite or weight?
turns.patient: Yes, actually... my appetite has been really off for the past five days. I've just not felt like eating much of anything, which is unusual for me because I usually love food. To be honest, it's been a bit concerning to me that I'm not hungry at all, so I've just been trying to stick to light meals and see how things go.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle — diet, exercise, daily routine.
turns.patient: I work as an elementary school teacher, so my day is pretty structured. I wake up early, have a quick breakfast, and head to school. My classes usually end around 3 pm, and then I spend some time grading papers or preparing for the next day's lessons. When I'm not teaching, I try to stay active by taking walks with my partner in the evenings or doing some light stretching exercises at home. As for diet, I'd say it's pretty normal - I eat a lot of fruits, vegetables, and whole grains. However, like I mentioned earlier, my appetite has been off lately, so I've just been sticking to what I feel like eating.

turns.student: How active are you physically? Do you work out or walk regularly?
turns.patient: I'd say I'm moderately active. As a teacher, I'm on my feet most of the day and do get some physical activity from walking around the classroom and dealing with energetic kids. When I'm not at school, I try to stay active by taking short walks with my partner in the evenings or doing some light stretching exercises at home. We don't really have a gym membership or anything like that, but we do enjoy going for longer hikes on weekends when the weather's nice. Lately, though, I've been feeling pretty fatigued and haven't had as much energy to be as active as I usually am.

turns.student: What does a typical day of eating look like for you?
turns.patient: Honestly, my appetite has been really off lately, so my typical day of eating hasn't been very consistent. Usually, I love food and enjoy trying out new recipes or cooking with my partner on weekends. But over the past five days, I've just not felt like eating much of anything. When I do eat, it's usually light meals like salads or soups that are easy to digest. I haven't been able to stick to my usual diet of fruits, vegetables, and whole grains because nothing seems appetizing to me right now. It's a bit concerning for me because I'm used to enjoying food so much.

turns.student: Any medical problems in your family? Parents, siblings?
turns.patient: Yes, my mother has asthma. She's been dealing with it for years, and she's always had to be mindful of her triggers and take medication regularly to manage her symptoms.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious diseases?
turns.patient: My mom's asthma is pretty manageable, but I'm worried that maybe there's something similar going on with me. She hasn't been diagnosed with anything else that I know of, but she does have to be careful about her breathing all the time.
